Category: Technology & Telecommunications: Information Technology, Science & Technology, Software, Hardware, Telecom
Description: Business impact when AI-dependent services fail.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how businesses use digital services day to day, along with a general awareness of what artificial intelligence tools are, is all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, learners will be able to identify the key causes of AI system outages and explain how service disruptions affect business operations across a range of industries. Learners will also apply practical strategies to assess risk, prioritise recovery actions, and communicate effectively with stakeholders when AI-dependent services become unavailable.

lessons.Description: This lesson explores what happens to businesses when the AI-powered services they rely on become unavailable, whether due to planned maintenance, unexpected outages, or technical failures. You will learn how to recognise the types of disruptions that can affect AI systems and understand why these interruptions can have significant consequences for day-to-day operations. We will look at real-world examples of how businesses have been impacted when critical AI tools go offline, from customer service platforms to automated workflows. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer picture of the risks involved in depending on AI-driven services and why it is important to plan for those risks from the outset.
lessons.content: Business impact when AI-dependent services fail.

slides.subtitle: Keeping AI services running reliably
slides.slidecontent:
• The degree to which an AI-powered service is accessible and functioning when users need it
• Measured as a percentage of uptime over a given period (for example, 99.9% availability)
• Even small drops in availability can disrupt business operations significantly
• Includes planned maintenance as well as unexpected failures
• Critical for any organisation that relies on AI tools to serve customers or run internal processes

slides.subtitle: Why AI systems fail
slides.slidecontent:
• Server overload from unexpected traffic spikes
• Software bugs in AI model updates or deployments
• Network connectivity issues affecting cloud-based services
• Third-party dependency failures (APIs, databases, cloud providers)
• Hardware failures in data centres
• Scheduled maintenance that runs longer than expected

slides.subtitle: When AI failures hit real businesses
slides.slidecontent:
• ChatGPT outages in 2023 left thousands of businesses unable to process customer queries for hours
• Salesforce Einstein AI disruptions blocked sales teams from accessing forecasts mid-quarter
• GitHub Copilot downtime forced developers to revert to manual coding, slowing sprint delivery
• Google Bard service interruptions affected marketing teams relying on AI-assisted content production

slides.subtitle: When AI fails, operations suffer most
slides.slidecontent:
• Orders stop processing immediately
• Staff lose key work tools
• Customers face long delays
• Revenue drops fast
• Trust erodes quickly

slides.subtitle: Beyond the obvious financial losses
slides.slidecontent:
• Lost revenue during the outage window, calculated per hour or per transaction
• Staff time spent on workarounds and incident response
• Reputational damage (harder to quantify, but very real)
• Customer churn if trust is broken
• Regulatory or contractual penalties for missed service level agreements
• Recovery costs including overtime, emergency support, and post-incident audits

slides.subtitle: Prioritising your AI dependencies
slides.slidecontent:
• Map all AI tools used across your organisation
• Classify each system by business criticality (high, medium, low)
• Identify single points of failure with no alternatives
• Calculate potential downtime costs for each system
• Assess frequency of past outages for each service
• Document which teams and processes would be affected most

slides.subtitle: Strategies to maintain business continuity
slides.slidecontent:
• Identify which business processes rely on AI tools and rank them by criticality
• Build manual fallback procedures before you need them
• Train staff on workarounds
• Keep offline copies of key templates, scripts, and reference materials that AI normally generates
• Set clear internal thresholds for when to escalate or switch to backup processes
• Test your continuity plan regularly, not just when something goes wrong

slides.subtitle: Managing stakeholder expectations
slides.slidecontent:
• Notify internal teams immediately when outages are detected
• Provide regular updates on restoration progress
• Be transparent about expected resolution times
• Explain alternative processes customers can use
• Document lessons learned for future incidents
• Follow up with affected stakeholders once service is restored

scripts.script: Let's start with a fundamental question: what do we actually mean by AI availability? In simple terms, it refers to how reliably an AI-powered service is up and running when people need to use it. We typically express this as a percentage of uptime, so you might hear figures like 99.9% availability, which sounds impressive until you realise that still allows for several hours of downtime each year. And even a short outage can cause real disruption, especially if your business depends on that AI tool to serve customers or keep internal processes moving. It is also worth noting that availability covers two different scenarios: planned maintenance, where the system is intentionally taken offline for updates, and unexpected failures, which nobody sees coming. So whether you are building AI systems or simply relying on them day to day, understanding availability is a genuinely important starting point for everything else we will cover in this course.

Now that we understand what availability means, let's look at why AI systems actually fail in the first place. Sometimes it's as simple as too many people trying to use the service at once, overwhelming the servers. Other times, it's a software bug that gets introduced when the AI model is updated or when new features are deployed. Network problems can also cut off access to cloud-based AI services, even if the AI itself is working fine. Many AI tools also depend on other services to function properly, so if those third-party APIs or databases go down, your AI tool goes with them. Hardware can fail too, especially in the data centres where these systems run. And finally, even planned maintenance can turn into an outage if it takes longer than expected or something goes wrong during the update process.

Let's look at some real examples of AI outages that have actually hurt businesses. In 2023, ChatGPT experienced several significant outages, and companies that relied on it to handle customer queries were simply stuck, sometimes for hours at a time. Salesforce Einstein, which many sales teams use for forecasting, also had disruptions, and when that happens mid-quarter, decisions get delayed and targets get missed. GitHub Copilot, the AI coding assistant, has gone down too, and developers who depend on it daily suddenly found themselves working much more slowly, which put real pressure on sprint deadlines. Even Google Bard had service interruptions that left marketing teams unable to produce content on schedule. And here is the important point running through all of these examples: this is not just an inconvenience. There is real money involved, real productivity lost, and real reputations at stake.

When an AI system goes down, the impact on day-to-day operations can be almost immediate. Think about an e-commerce business, for example. The moment that AI-powered order processing fails, transactions simply grind to a halt. Staff who rely on AI tools to do their jobs are suddenly left without the support they need, which slows everything down further. Meanwhile, customers are waiting longer than usual, and they notice. They get frustrated. And when customers are frustrated, they spend less, which means revenue starts to fall quite quickly. But perhaps the most damaging consequence is harder to measure in the short term, and that is trust. Once a customer has a poor experience because of a system failure, it can take a long time to win them back.

When an AI system goes down, the financial impact goes well beyond what you might first expect. Yes, there's the obvious lost revenue for every hour the system isn't working, but that's really just the starting point. You also have staff scrambling to find workarounds, which burns time and productivity. Then there's the reputational side of things, which is harder to put a number on but can be genuinely damaging, especially if customers lose confidence and decide to take their business elsewhere. On top of that, if you have service level agreements in place, missing those targets can trigger penalties or even legal consequences. And once the outage is over, the costs don't stop there either. You're looking at overtime, emergency support, and thorough post-incident reviews to understand what went wrong.

Before you can effectively manage AI outages, you need to understand exactly what you're dealing with. Start by creating a comprehensive map of every AI tool your organisation uses, from customer-facing chatbots to internal automation systems. Then classify each one by how critical it is to your business operations. Ask yourself: if this system went down for an hour, a day, or a week, what would happen? Some tools might be convenient but not essential, while others could bring your entire operation to a standstill. Pay special attention to systems where you have no alternative, because these represent your highest risk. Finally, try to put some numbers on this. What would each hour of downtime actually cost you in lost revenue, wasted time, or missed opportunities?

So, now that we understand how outages happen and what they cost, let's talk about what you can actually do to prepare for them. The starting point is knowing which of your business processes depend on AI tools, and being honest about which ones would cause the most disruption if that AI suddenly became unavailable. Once you know that, you can build manual workarounds in advance, and crucially, make sure your team knows how to use them before there's a crisis. It's also worth keeping offline copies of things like templates, scripts, or reference documents that you'd normally ask an AI to produce for you. Beyond that, agree internally on clear trigger points, so everyone knows at what stage to escalate or switch to a backup process.

When an AI outage does occur, how you communicate can make the difference between a manageable incident and a reputation disaster. Start by alerting your internal teams immediately, even if you don't have all the details yet. People need to know there's a problem so they can start implementing backup processes. Keep everyone updated regularly on your progress toward fixing the issue, and be realistic about timelines rather than overly optimistic. For external stakeholders, transparency builds trust even in difficult situations. Explain what alternative processes they can use while you work on the problem. And once everything is back to normal, follow up with the people who were affected, acknowledge the disruption, and share what you've learned to prevent similar issues in future.

Who is this for: Business managers, operations managers, project managers, IT managers, customer service managers, business analysts, team leads, department heads
Team or Dept this is for: Operations, IT, Business Operations, Customer Service, Risk Management, Business Continuity
